lines: The Duke of Buckingham's surveyor, ha?
Where's his examination?
fullText: The Duke of Buckingham's surveyor, ha? Where's his examination?

lines: Is he in person ready?
fullText: Is he in person ready?

lines: Well, we shall then know more, and Buckingham
Shall lessen this big look.
fullText: Well, we shall then know more, and Buckingham Shall lessen this big look.

lines: Please you, sir,
I know but of a single part in aught
Pertains to th' state, and front but in that file
Where others tell steps with me.
fullText: Please you, sir, I know but of a single part in aught Pertains to th' state, and front but in that file Where others tell steps with me.

lines: And for me,
I have no further gone in this than by
A single voice, and that not passed me but
By learned approbation of the judges. If I am
Traduced by ignorant tongues, which neither know
My faculties nor person, yet will be
The chronicles of my doing, let me say
'Tis but the fate of place, and the rough brake
That virtue must go through. We must not stint
Our necessary actions in the fear
To cope malicious censurers, which ever,
As ravenous fishes, do a vessel follow
That is new trimmed, but benefit no further
Than vainly longing. What we oft do best,
By sick interpreters, once weak ones, is
Not ours or not allowed; what worst, as oft,
Hitting a grosser quality, is cried up
For our best act. If we shall stand still
In fear our motion will be mocked or carped at,
We should take root here where we sit,
Or sit state-statues only.
fullText: And for me, I have no further gone in this than by A single voice, and that not passed me but By learned approbation of the judges. If I am Traduced by ignorant tongues, which neither know My faculties nor person, yet will be The chronicles of my doing, let me say 'Tis but the fate of place, and the rough brake That virtue must go through. We must not stint Our necessary actions in the fear To cope malicious censurers, which ever, As ravenous fishes, do a vessel follow That is new trimmed, but benefit no further Than vainly longing. What we oft do best, By sick interpreters, once weak ones, is Not ours or not allowed; what worst, as oft, Hitting a grosser quality, is cried up For our best act. If we shall stand still In fear our motion will be mocked or carped at, We should take root here where we sit, Or sit state-statues only.

lines: A word with you.
Let there be letters writ to every shire
Of the King's grace and pardon. The grieved commons
Hardly conceive of me. Let it be noised
That through our intercession this revokement
And pardon comes. I shall anon advise you
Further in the proceeding.
fullText: A word with you. Let there be letters writ to every shire Of the King's grace and pardon. The grieved commons Hardly conceive of me. Let it be noised That through our intercession this revokement And pardon comes. I shall anon advise you Further in the proceeding.

lines: Stand forth, and with bold spirit relate what you
Most like a careful subject have collected
Out of the Duke of Buckingham.
fullText: Stand forth, and with bold spirit relate what you Most like a careful subject have collected Out of the Duke of Buckingham.

lines: Please your Highness, note
This dangerous conception in this point:
Not friended by his wish to your high person,
His will is most malignant, and it stretches
Beyond you to your friends.
fullText: Please your Highness, note This dangerous conception in this point: Not friended by his wish to your high person, His will is most malignant, and it stretches Beyond you to your friends.

lines: Now, madam, may his Highness live in freedom
And this man out of prison?
fullText: Now, madam, may his Highness live in freedom And this man out of prison?

lines: You're welcome, my fair guests. That noble lady
Or gentleman that is not freely merry
Is not my friend. This to confirm my welcome,
And to you all good health.
fullText: You're welcome, my fair guests. That noble lady Or gentleman that is not freely merry Is not my friend. This to confirm my welcome, And to you all good health.

lines: My Lord Sands,
I am beholding to you. Cheer your neighbors.--
Ladies, you are not merry.--Gentlemen,
Whose fault is this?
fullText: My Lord Sands, I am beholding to you. Cheer your neighbors.-- Ladies, you are not merry.--Gentlemen, Whose fault is this?

lines: What's that?
fullText: What's that?

lines: What warlike voice,
And to what end, is this?--Nay, ladies, fear not.
By all the laws of war you're privileged.
fullText: What warlike voice, And to what end, is this?--Nay, ladies, fear not. By all the laws of war you're privileged.

lines: Good Lord Chamberlain,
Go, give 'em welcome--you can speak the French
tongue--
And pray receive 'em nobly, and conduct 'em
Into our presence, where this heaven of beauty
Shall shine at full upon them. Some attend him.
You have now a broken banquet, but we'll mend it.
A good digestion to you all; and once more
I shower a welcome on you. Welcome all!
fullText: Good Lord Chamberlain, Go, give 'em welcome--you can speak the French tongue-- And pray receive 'em nobly, and conduct 'em Into our presence, where this heaven of beauty Shall shine at full upon them. Some attend him. You have now a broken banquet, but we'll mend it. A good digestion to you all; and once more I shower a welcome on you. Welcome all!

lines: Say, Lord Chamberlain,
They have done my poor house grace, for which I
pay 'em
A thousand thanks and pray 'em take their pleasures.
King chooses Anne Bullen.]
fullText: Say, Lord Chamberlain, They have done my poor house grace, for which I pay 'em A thousand thanks and pray 'em take their pleasures. King chooses Anne Bullen.]

lines: My lord!
fullText: My lord!

lines: Pray tell 'em thus much
from me:
There should be one amongst 'em by his person
More worthy this place than myself, to whom,
If I but knew him, with my love and duty
I would surrender it.
fullText: Pray tell 'em thus much from me: There should be one amongst 'em by his person More worthy this place than myself, to whom, If I but knew him, with my love and duty I would surrender it.

lines: What say they?
fullText: What say they?

lines: Let me see, then.
By all your good leaves, gentlemen.
Here I'll make
My royal choice.
fullText: Let me see, then. By all your good leaves, gentlemen. Here I'll make My royal choice.

lines: I am glad
Your Grace is grown so pleasant.
fullText: I am glad Your Grace is grown so pleasant.

lines: Sir Thomas Lovell, is the banquet ready
I' th' privy chamber?
fullText: Sir Thomas Lovell, is the banquet ready I' th' privy chamber?

lines: Your Grace,
I fear, with dancing is a little heated.
fullText: Your Grace, I fear, with dancing is a little heated.

lines: There's fresher air, my lord,
In the next chamber.
fullText: There's fresher air, my lord, In the next chamber.

lines: Sir, you cannot.
I would your Grace would give us but an hour
Of private conference.
fullText: Sir, you cannot. I would your Grace would give us but an hour Of private conference.

lines: Your Grace has given a precedent of wisdom
Above all princes in committing freely
Your scruple to the voice of Christendom.
Who can be angry now? What envy reach you?
The Spaniard, tied by blood and favor to her,
Must now confess, if they have any goodness,
The trial just and noble; all the clerks--
I mean the learned ones in Christian kingdoms--
Have their free voices; Rome, the nurse of judgment,
Invited by your noble self, hath sent
One general tongue unto us, this good man,
This just and learned priest, Cardinal Campeius,
Whom once more I present unto your Highness.
fullText: Your Grace has given a precedent of wisdom Above all princes in committing freely Your scruple to the voice of Christendom. Who can be angry now? What envy reach you? The Spaniard, tied by blood and favor to her, Must now confess, if they have any goodness, The trial just and noble; all the clerks-- I mean the learned ones in Christian kingdoms-- Have their free voices; Rome, the nurse of judgment, Invited by your noble self, hath sent One general tongue unto us, this good man, This just and learned priest, Cardinal Campeius, Whom once more I present unto your Highness.

lines: I know your Majesty has always loved her
So dear in heart not to deny her that
A woman of less place might ask by law:
Scholars allowed freely to argue for her.
fullText: I know your Majesty has always loved her So dear in heart not to deny her that A woman of less place might ask by law: Scholars allowed freely to argue for her.

lines: Give me your hand. Much joy and favor to you.
You are the King's now.
fullText: Give me your hand. Much joy and favor to you. You are the King's now.

lines: Yes, he was.
fullText: Yes, he was.

lines: Yes, surely.
fullText: Yes, surely.

lines: How? Of me?
fullText: How? Of me?

lines: Heav'n's peace be with him!
That's Christian care enough. For living murmurers,
There's places of rebuke. He was a fool,
For he would needs be virtuous. That good fellow
If I command him follows my appointment.
I will have none so near else. Learn this, brother:
We live not to be griped by meaner persons.
fullText: Heav'n's peace be with him! That's Christian care enough. For living murmurers, There's places of rebuke. He was a fool, For he would needs be virtuous. That good fellow If I command him follows my appointment. I will have none so near else. Learn this, brother: We live not to be griped by meaner persons.

lines: Whilst our commission from Rome is read,
Let silence be commanded.
fullText: Whilst our commission from Rome is read, Let silence be commanded.

lines: Be 't so. Proceed.
fullText: Be 't so. Proceed.

lines: You have here, lady,
And of your choice, these reverend fathers, men
Of singular integrity and learning,
Yea, the elect o' th' land, who are assembled
To plead your cause. It shall be therefore bootless
That longer you desire the court, as well
For your own quiet as to rectify
What is unsettled in the King.
fullText: You have here, lady, And of your choice, these reverend fathers, men Of singular integrity and learning, Yea, the elect o' th' land, who are assembled To plead your cause. It shall be therefore bootless That longer you desire the court, as well For your own quiet as to rectify What is unsettled in the King.

lines: Your pleasure, madam.
fullText: Your pleasure, madam.

lines: Be patient yet.
fullText: Be patient yet.

lines: I do profess
You speak not like yourself, who ever yet
Have stood to charity and displayed th' effects
Of disposition gentle and of wisdom
O'ertopping woman's power. Madam, you do me
wrong.
I have no spleen against you, nor injustice
For you or any. How far I have proceeded,
Or how far further shall, is warranted
By a commission from the Consistory,
Yea, the whole Consistory of Rome. You charge me
That I "have blown this coal." I do deny it.
The King is present. If it be known to him
That I gainsay my deed, how may he wound,
And worthily, my falsehood, yea, as much
As you have done my truth. If he know
That I am free of your report, he knows
I am not of your wrong. Therefore in him
It lies to cure me, and the cure is to
Remove these thoughts from you, the which before
His Highness shall speak in, I do beseech
You, gracious madam, to unthink your speaking
And to say so no more.
fullText: I do profess You speak not like yourself, who ever yet Have stood to charity and displayed th' effects Of disposition gentle and of wisdom O'ertopping woman's power. Madam, you do me wrong. I have no spleen against you, nor injustice For you or any. How far I have proceeded, Or how far further shall, is warranted By a commission from the Consistory, Yea, the whole Consistory of Rome. You charge me That I "have blown this coal." I do deny it. The King is present. If it be known to him That I gainsay my deed, how may he wound, And worthily, my falsehood, yea, as much As you have done my truth. If he know That I am free of your report, he knows I am not of your wrong. Therefore in him It lies to cure me, and the cure is to Remove these thoughts from you, the which before His Highness shall speak in, I do beseech You, gracious madam, to unthink your speaking And to say so no more.

lines: Most gracious sir,
In humblest manner I require your Highness
That it shall please you to declare in hearing
Of all these ears--for where I am robbed and bound,
There must I be unloosed, although not there
At once and fully satisfied--whether ever I
Did broach this business to your Highness, or
Laid any scruple in your way which might
Induce you to the question on 't, or ever
Have to you, but with thanks to God for such
A royal lady, spake one the least word that might
Be to the prejudice of her present state,
Or touch of her good person?
fullText: Most gracious sir, In humblest manner I require your Highness That it shall please you to declare in hearing Of all these ears--for where I am robbed and bound, There must I be unloosed, although not there At once and fully satisfied--whether ever I Did broach this business to your Highness, or Laid any scruple in your way which might Induce you to the question on 't, or ever Have to you, but with thanks to God for such A royal lady, spake one the least word that might Be to the prejudice of her present state, Or touch of her good person?

lines: Peace to your Highness.
fullText: Peace to your Highness.

lines: May it please you, noble madam, to withdraw
Into your private chamber, we shall give you
The full cause of our coming.
fullText: May it please you, noble madam, to withdraw Into your private chamber, we shall give you The full cause of our coming.

lines: Tanta est erga te mentis integritas, regina
serenissima--
fullText: Tanta est erga te mentis integritas, regina serenissima--

lines: Noble lady,
I am sorry my integrity should breed--
And service to his Majesty and you--
So deep suspicion, where all faith was meant.
We come not by the way of accusation,
To taint that honor every good tongue blesses,
Nor to betray you any way to sorrow--
You have too much, good lady--but to know
How you stand minded in the weighty difference
Between the King and you, and to deliver,
Like free and honest men, our just opinions
And comforts to your cause.
fullText: Noble lady, I am sorry my integrity should breed-- And service to his Majesty and you-- So deep suspicion, where all faith was meant. We come not by the way of accusation, To taint that honor every good tongue blesses, Nor to betray you any way to sorrow-- You have too much, good lady--but to know How you stand minded in the weighty difference Between the King and you, and to deliver, Like free and honest men, our just opinions And comforts to your cause.

lines: Madam, you wrong the King's love with these fears;
Your hopes and friends are infinite.
fullText: Madam, you wrong the King's love with these fears; Your hopes and friends are infinite.

lines: He tells you rightly.
fullText: He tells you rightly.

lines: Madam, this is a mere distraction.
You turn the good we offer into envy.
fullText: Madam, this is a mere distraction. You turn the good we offer into envy.

lines: Madam, you wander from the good we aim at.
fullText: Madam, you wander from the good we aim at.

lines: Pray hear me.
fullText: Pray hear me.

lines: If your Grace
Could but be brought to know our ends are honest,
You'd feel more comfort. Why should we, good lady,
Upon what cause, wrong you? Alas, our places,
The way of our profession, is against it.
We are to cure such sorrows, not to sow 'em.
For goodness' sake, consider what you do,
How you may hurt yourself, ay, utterly
Grow from the King's acquaintance by this carriage.
The hearts of princes kiss obedience,
So much they love it. But to stubborn spirits
They swell and grow as terrible as storms.
I know you have a gentle, noble temper,
A soul as even as a calm. Pray think us
Those we profess: peacemakers, friends, and servants.
fullText: If your Grace Could but be brought to know our ends are honest, You'd feel more comfort. Why should we, good lady, Upon what cause, wrong you? Alas, our places, The way of our profession, is against it. We are to cure such sorrows, not to sow 'em. For goodness' sake, consider what you do, How you may hurt yourself, ay, utterly Grow from the King's acquaintance by this carriage. The hearts of princes kiss obedience, So much they love it. But to stubborn spirits They swell and grow as terrible as storms. I know you have a gentle, noble temper, A soul as even as a calm. Pray think us Those we profess: peacemakers, friends, and servants.

lines: The packet, Cromwell;
Gave 't you the King?
fullText: The packet, Cromwell; Gave 't you the King?

lines: Looked he o' th' inside of the paper?
fullText: Looked he o' th' inside of the paper?

lines: Is he ready
To come abroad?
fullText: Is he ready To come abroad?

lines: Leave me awhile.
The French king's sister; he shall marry her.
Anne Bullen? No, I'll no Anne Bullens for him.
There's more in 't than fair visage. Bullen?
No, we'll no Bullens. Speedily I wish
To hear from Rome. The Marchioness of Pembroke!
fullText: Leave me awhile. The French king's sister; he shall marry her. Anne Bullen? No, I'll no Anne Bullens for him. There's more in 't than fair visage. Bullen? No, we'll no Bullens. Speedily I wish To hear from Rome. The Marchioness of Pembroke!

lines: The late queen's gentlewoman, a knight's daughter,
To be her mistress' mistress? The Queen's queen?
This candle burns not clear. 'Tis I must snuff it;
Then out it goes. What though I know her virtuous
And well-deserving? Yet I know her for
A spleeny Lutheran, and not wholesome to
Our cause that she should lie i' th' bosom of
Our hard-ruled king. Again, there is sprung up
An heretic, an arch-one, Cranmer, one
Hath crawled into the favor of the King
And is his oracle.
fullText: The late queen's gentlewoman, a knight's daughter, To be her mistress' mistress? The Queen's queen? This candle burns not clear. 'Tis I must snuff it; Then out it goes. What though I know her virtuous And well-deserving? Yet I know her for A spleeny Lutheran, and not wholesome to Our cause that she should lie i' th' bosom of Our hard-ruled king. Again, there is sprung up An heretic, an arch-one, Cranmer, one Hath crawled into the favor of the King And is his oracle.

lines: Heaven forgive me!
Ever God bless your Highness.
fullText: Heaven forgive me! Ever God bless your Highness.

lines: Sir,
For holy offices I have a time; a time
To think upon the part of business which
I bear i' th' state; and Nature does require
Her times of preservation, which perforce
I, her frail son, amongst my brethren mortal,
Must give my tendance to.
fullText: Sir, For holy offices I have a time; a time To think upon the part of business which I bear i' th' state; and Nature does require Her times of preservation, which perforce I, her frail son, amongst my brethren mortal, Must give my tendance to.

lines: And ever may your Highness yoke together,
As I will lend you cause, my doing well
With my well saying.
fullText: And ever may your Highness yoke together, As I will lend you cause, my doing well With my well saying.

lines: What should this mean?
fullText: What should this mean?

lines: My sovereign, I confess your royal graces,
Showered on me daily, have been more than could
My studied purposes requite, which went
Beyond all man's endeavors. My endeavors
Have ever come too short of my desires,
Yet filed with my abilities. Mine own ends
Have been mine so, that evermore they pointed
To th' good of your most sacred person and
The profit of the state. For your great graces
Heaped upon me, poor undeserver, I
Can nothing render but allegiant thanks,
My prayers to heaven for you, my loyalty,
Which ever has and ever shall be growing
Till death--that winter--kill it.
fullText: My sovereign, I confess your royal graces, Showered on me daily, have been more than could My studied purposes requite, which went Beyond all man's endeavors. My endeavors Have ever come too short of my desires, Yet filed with my abilities. Mine own ends Have been mine so, that evermore they pointed To th' good of your most sacred person and The profit of the state. For your great graces Heaped upon me, poor undeserver, I Can nothing render but allegiant thanks, My prayers to heaven for you, my loyalty, Which ever has and ever shall be growing Till death--that winter--kill it.

lines: I do profess
That for your Highness' good I ever labored
More than mine own, that am, have, and will be--
Though all the world should crack their duty to you
And throw it from their soul, though perils did
Abound as thick as thought could make 'em, and
Appear in forms more horrid--yet my duty,
As doth a rock against the chiding flood,
Should the approach of this wild river break,
And stand unshaken yours.
fullText: I do profess That for your Highness' good I ever labored More than mine own, that am, have, and will be-- Though all the world should crack their duty to you And throw it from their soul, though perils did Abound as thick as thought could make 'em, and Appear in forms more horrid--yet my duty, As doth a rock against the chiding flood, Should the approach of this wild river break, And stand unshaken yours.

lines: What should this mean?
What sudden anger's this? How have I reaped it?
He parted frowning from me, as if ruin
Leaped from his eyes. So looks the chafed lion
Upon the daring huntsman that has galled him,
Then makes him nothing. I must read this paper--
I fear, the story of his anger.
'Tis so.
This paper has undone me. 'Tis th' accompt
Of all that world of wealth I have drawn together
For mine own ends--indeed, to gain the popedom
And fee my friends in Rome. O negligence,
Fit for a fool to fall by! What cross devil
Made me put this main secret in the packet
I sent the King? Is there no way to cure this?
No new device to beat this from his brains?
I know 'twill stir him strongly; yet I know
A way, if it take right, in spite of fortune
Will bring me off again.
What's this? "To th' Pope"?
The letter, as I live, with all the business
I writ to 's Holiness. Nay then, farewell!
I have touched the highest point of all my greatness,
And from that full meridian of my glory
I haste now to my setting. I shall fall
Like a bright exhalation in the evening
And no man see me more.
fullText: What should this mean? What sudden anger's this? How have I reaped it? He parted frowning from me, as if ruin Leaped from his eyes. So looks the chafed lion Upon the daring huntsman that has galled him, Then makes him nothing. I must read this paper-- I fear, the story of his anger. 'Tis so. This paper has undone me. 'Tis th' accompt Of all that world of wealth I have drawn together For mine own ends--indeed, to gain the popedom And fee my friends in Rome. O negligence, Fit for a fool to fall by! What cross devil Made me put this main secret in the packet I sent the King? Is there no way to cure this? No new device to beat this from his brains? I know 'twill stir him strongly; yet I know A way, if it take right, in spite of fortune Will bring me off again. What's this? "To th' Pope"? The letter, as I live, with all the business I writ to 's Holiness. Nay then, farewell! I have touched the highest point of all my greatness, And from that full meridian of my glory I haste now to my setting. I shall fall Like a bright exhalation in the evening And no man see me more.

lines: Stay.
Where's your commission, lords? Words cannot carry
Authority so weighty.
fullText: Stay. Where's your commission, lords? Words cannot carry Authority so weighty.

lines: Till I find more than will or words to do it--
I mean your malice--know, officious lords,
I dare and must deny it. Now I feel
Of what coarse metal you are molded, envy;
How eagerly you follow my disgraces,
As if it fed you, and how sleek and wanton
You appear in everything may bring my ruin.
Follow your envious courses, men of malice;
You have Christian warrant for 'em, and no doubt
In time will find their fit rewards. That seal
You ask with such a violence, the King,
Mine and your master, with his own hand gave me;
Bade me enjoy it, with the place and honors,
During my life; and to confirm his goodness,
Tied it by letters patents. Now, who'll take it?
fullText: Till I find more than will or words to do it-- I mean your malice--know, officious lords, I dare and must deny it. Now I feel Of what coarse metal you are molded, envy; How eagerly you follow my disgraces, As if it fed you, and how sleek and wanton You appear in everything may bring my ruin. Follow your envious courses, men of malice; You have Christian warrant for 'em, and no doubt In time will find their fit rewards. That seal You ask with such a violence, the King, Mine and your master, with his own hand gave me; Bade me enjoy it, with the place and honors, During my life; and to confirm his goodness, Tied it by letters patents. Now, who'll take it?

lines: It must be himself, then.
fullText: It must be himself, then.

lines: Proud lord, thou liest.
Within these forty hours Surrey durst better
Have burnt that tongue than said so.
fullText: Proud lord, thou liest. Within these forty hours Surrey durst better Have burnt that tongue than said so.

lines: This, and all else
This talking lord can lay upon my credit,
I answer, is most false. The Duke by law
Found his deserts. How innocent I was
From any private malice in his end,
His noble jury and foul cause can witness.--
If I loved many words, lord, I should tell you
You have as little honesty as honor,
That in the way of loyalty and truth
Toward the King, my ever royal master,
Dare mate a sounder man than Surrey can be,
And all that love his follies.
fullText: This, and all else This talking lord can lay upon my credit, I answer, is most false. The Duke by law Found his deserts. How innocent I was From any private malice in his end, His noble jury and foul cause can witness.-- If I loved many words, lord, I should tell you You have as little honesty as honor, That in the way of loyalty and truth Toward the King, my ever royal master, Dare mate a sounder man than Surrey can be, And all that love his follies.

lines: All goodness
Is poison to thy stomach.
fullText: All goodness Is poison to thy stomach.

lines: How much, methinks, I could despise this man,
But that I am bound in charity against it!
fullText: How much, methinks, I could despise this man, But that I am bound in charity against it!

lines: So much fairer
And spotless shall mine innocence arise
When the King knows my truth.
fullText: So much fairer And spotless shall mine innocence arise When the King knows my truth.

lines: Speak on, sir.
I dare your worst objections. If I blush,
It is to see a nobleman want manners.
fullText: Speak on, sir. I dare your worst objections. If I blush, It is to see a nobleman want manners.

lines: So, farewell to the little good you bear me.
Farewell? A long farewell to all my greatness!
This is the state of man: today he puts forth
The tender leaves of hopes; tomorrow blossoms
And bears his blushing honors thick upon him;
The third day comes a frost, a killing frost,
And when he thinks, good easy man, full surely
His greatness is a-ripening, nips his root,
And then he falls, as I do. I have ventured,
Like little wanton boys that swim on bladders,
This many summers in a sea of glory,
But far beyond my depth. My high-blown pride
At length broke under me and now has left me,
Weary and old with service, to the mercy
Of a rude stream that must forever hide me.
Vain pomp and glory of this world, I hate you.
I feel my heart new opened. O, how wretched
Is that poor man that hangs on princes' favors!
There is betwixt that smile we would aspire to,
That sweet aspect of princes, and their ruin,
More pangs and fears than wars or women have;
And when he falls, he falls like Lucifer,
Never to hope again.
fullText: So, farewell to the little good you bear me. Farewell? A long farewell to all my greatness! This is the state of man: today he puts forth The tender leaves of hopes; tomorrow blossoms And bears his blushing honors thick upon him; The third day comes a frost, a killing frost, And when he thinks, good easy man, full surely His greatness is a-ripening, nips his root, And then he falls, as I do. I have ventured, Like little wanton boys that swim on bladders, This many summers in a sea of glory, But far beyond my depth. My high-blown pride At length broke under me and now has left me, Weary and old with service, to the mercy Of a rude stream that must forever hide me. Vain pomp and glory of this world, I hate you. I feel my heart new opened. O, how wretched Is that poor man that hangs on princes' favors! There is betwixt that smile we would aspire to, That sweet aspect of princes, and their ruin, More pangs and fears than wars or women have; And when he falls, he falls like Lucifer, Never to hope again.

lines: What, amazed
At my misfortunes? Can thy spirit wonder
A great man should decline? Nay, an you weep,
I am fall'n indeed.
fullText: What, amazed At my misfortunes? Can thy spirit wonder A great man should decline? Nay, an you weep, I am fall'n indeed.

lines: Why, well.
Never so truly happy, my good Cromwell.
I know myself now, and I feel within me
A peace above all earthly dignities,
A still and quiet conscience. The King has cured me--
I humbly thank his Grace--and from these shoulders,
These ruined pillars, out of pity, taken
A load would sink a navy: too much honor.
O, 'tis a burden, Cromwell, 'tis a burden
Too heavy for a man that hopes for heaven.
fullText: Why, well. Never so truly happy, my good Cromwell. I know myself now, and I feel within me A peace above all earthly dignities, A still and quiet conscience. The King has cured me-- I humbly thank his Grace--and from these shoulders, These ruined pillars, out of pity, taken A load would sink a navy: too much honor. O, 'tis a burden, Cromwell, 'tis a burden Too heavy for a man that hopes for heaven.

lines: I hope I have. I am able now, methinks,
Out of a fortitude of soul I feel,
To endure more miseries and greater far
Than my weak-hearted enemies dare offer.
What news abroad?
fullText: I hope I have. I am able now, methinks, Out of a fortitude of soul I feel, To endure more miseries and greater far Than my weak-hearted enemies dare offer. What news abroad?

lines: God bless him.
fullText: God bless him.

lines: That's somewhat sudden.
But he's a learned man. May he continue
Long in his Highness' favor and do justice
For truth's sake and his conscience, that his bones,
When he has run his course and sleeps in blessings,
May have a tomb of orphans' tears wept on him.
What more?
fullText: That's somewhat sudden. But he's a learned man. May he continue Long in his Highness' favor and do justice For truth's sake and his conscience, that his bones, When he has run his course and sleeps in blessings, May have a tomb of orphans' tears wept on him. What more?

lines: That's news indeed.
fullText: That's news indeed.

lines: There was the weight that pulled me down.
O Cromwell,
The King has gone beyond me. All my glories
In that one woman I have lost forever.
No sun shall ever usher forth mine honors,
Or gild again the noble troops that waited
Upon my smiles. Go, get thee from me, Cromwell.
I am a poor fall'n man, unworthy now
To be thy lord and master. Seek the King;
That sun, I pray, may never set! I have told him
What and how true thou art. He will advance thee;
Some little memory of me will stir him--
I know his noble nature--not to let
Thy hopeful service perish too. Good Cromwell,
Neglect him not. Make use now, and provide
For thine own future safety.
fullText: There was the weight that pulled me down. O Cromwell, The King has gone beyond me. All my glories In that one woman I have lost forever. No sun shall ever usher forth mine honors, Or gild again the noble troops that waited Upon my smiles. Go, get thee from me, Cromwell. I am a poor fall'n man, unworthy now To be thy lord and master. Seek the King; That sun, I pray, may never set! I have told him What and how true thou art. He will advance thee; Some little memory of me will stir him-- I know his noble nature--not to let Thy hopeful service perish too. Good Cromwell, Neglect him not. Make use now, and provide For thine own future safety.

lines: Cromwell, I did not think to shed a tear
In all my miseries, but thou hast forced me,
Out of thy honest truth, to play the woman.
Let's dry our eyes. And thus far hear me, Cromwell,
And when I am forgotten, as I shall be,
And sleep in dull cold marble, where no mention
Of me more must be heard of, say I taught thee;
Say Wolsey, that once trod the ways of glory
And sounded all the depths and shoals of honor,
Found thee a way, out of his wrack, to rise in,
A sure and safe one, though thy master missed it.
Mark but my fall and that that ruined me.
Cromwell, I charge thee, fling away ambition!
By that sin fell the angels; how can man, then,
The image of his maker, hope to win by it?
Love thyself last; cherish those hearts that hate thee.
Corruption wins not more than honesty.
Still in thy right hand carry gentle peace
To silence envious tongues. Be just, and fear not.
Let all the ends thou aim'st at be thy country's,
Thy God's, and truth's. Then if thou fall'st, O Cromwell,
Thou fall'st a blessed martyr.
Serve the King. And, prithee, lead me in.
There take an inventory of all I have
To the last penny; 'tis the King's. My robe
And my integrity to heaven is all
I dare now call mine own. O Cromwell, Cromwell,
Had I but served my God with half the zeal
I served my king, He would not in mine age
Have left me naked to mine enemies.
fullText: Cromwell, I did not think to shed a tear In all my miseries, but thou hast forced me, Out of thy honest truth, to play the woman. Let's dry our eyes. And thus far hear me, Cromwell, And when I am forgotten, as I shall be, And sleep in dull cold marble, where no mention Of me more must be heard of, say I taught thee; Say Wolsey, that once trod the ways of glory And sounded all the depths and shoals of honor, Found thee a way, out of his wrack, to rise in, A sure and safe one, though thy master missed it. Mark but my fall and that that ruined me. Cromwell, I charge thee, fling away ambition! By that sin fell the angels; how can man, then, The image of his maker, hope to win by it? Love thyself last; cherish those hearts that hate thee. Corruption wins not more than honesty. Still in thy right hand carry gentle peace To silence envious tongues. Be just, and fear not. Let all the ends thou aim'st at be thy country's, Thy God's, and truth's. Then if thou fall'st, O Cromwell, Thou fall'st a blessed martyr. Serve the King. And, prithee, lead me in. There take an inventory of all I have To the last penny; 'tis the King's. My robe And my integrity to heaven is all I dare now call mine own. O Cromwell, Cromwell, Had I but served my God with half the zeal I served my king, He would not in mine age Have left me naked to mine enemies.

lines: So I have. Farewell,
The hopes of court! My hopes in heaven do dwell.
fullText: So I have. Farewell, The hopes of court! My hopes in heaven do dwell.
